title: 어서 오세요

goal.en: I can welcome guests and greet people politely

key_points: 어서 오세요 / 들어 오세요 — welcoming someone
덕분에 잘 지내고 있어요 — I've been well, thanks to you
~고 있어요 vs ~고 계세요 — progressive (casual vs honorific)
인사해야지 — you should greet / mild suggestion

script.speaker: mirae_mom
script.en: Thank you so much for the invitation.

script.speaker: mirae_mom
script.en: I've been doing well, thanks to you.

script.speaker: liam
script.en: Are Mirae and Ria doing well too?

script.speaker: mirae_mom
script.text: 그럼~ 잘 있어. 한 번 놀러 와.
script.en: Of course! They're fine. Come visit sometime~

items.english: ma'am / aunt (address term for adult woman)
sir / uncle (address term for adult man)
thanks to you / owing to you
to be doing well / is doing well
to come to visit/hang out / comes to visit

grammar.title: 계세요 — 어른에게 쓰는 진행형
grammar.rule: 어른/윗사람의 동작을 표현할 때: -고 있어요 → -고 계세요 · Use -고 계세요 for elders/superiors
grammar.pattern: 동사 stem + 고 있어요 / 고 계세요
grammar.explanation_en: Both mean 'is doing / is in the middle of doing.' Use ~고 있어요 for friends and family (casual), and ~고 계세요 when talking about or to an elder (honorific). The same way you'd use -셨어요 for past, use -고 계세요 for present continuous.

examples.en: I've been doing well. (~고 있어요, about myself)
What is the teacher doing right now? (~고 계세요, honorific)
Liam is doing homework. (~고 있어요, about a peer)

grammar.title: 어서 오세요 / 들어 오세요
grammar.rule: 어서 오세요 = Welcome! (at the door) · 들어 오세요 = Please come in (inviting inside)
grammar.explanation_en: Korean has two distinct phrases when welcoming a guest. 어서 오세요 is said first when they arrive at the door, and 들어 오세요 invites them inside. Both use -세요, the polite imperative form.

examples.en: Welcome! So glad you came.
Please come in. Please have a seat.
Welcome! I've been waiting for you!

fill_blanks.hint: 손님이 도착했을 때 문 앞에서 → 어서 오세요
안으로 들어오라고 할 때 → 들어 오세요

grammar.title: -(으)셨어요 — 높임 과거
grammar.rule: 동사 stem + -(으)셨어요 → 어른을 높여서 말할 때
grammar.pattern: 지내다 → 지내셨어요? · 오다 → 오셨어요 · 먹다 → 드셨어요
grammar.explanation_en: When asking about or referring to someone older or of higher status, add -(으)셨어요 instead of plain -었어요/-았어요. This shows respect. Think of it as the 'polite upgrade' for past/present.

examples.en: How have you been? (polite)
I'm glad you came. (polite)
Have you eaten? (polite)

grammar.title: 덕분에 잘 지내고 있어요
grammar.title_en: I've Been Well, Thanks to You
grammar.rule: 덕분에 = thanks to you · 잘 지내다 = to be doing well
grammar.explanation_en: 덕분에 is a warm, humble phrase meaning 'thanks to you' or 'owing to your grace.' Koreans often use this instead of a plain 'I'm fine' — it credits the other person for your wellbeing. Very polite and warm.

examples.en: I've been doing well, thanks to you.
Thanks to my teacher, I'm good at Korean.

examples.ko: A: 요즘 어때요? B: 덕분에 좋아요!
examples.en: A: How are you these days? B: I'm good, thanks to you!

fill_blanks.hint: 'thanks to you / owing to you' = 덕분에

grammar.title: ~아/어야지 — 해야지?
grammar.rule: 동사 stem + 아/어야지 → 부드러운 권유 · gentle push (you should...!)
grammar.explanation_en: ~아/어야지 is a softer way of saying 'you should' or 'you've gotta.' It sounds like a gentle reminder — a parent nudging a child, or a friend reminding you of something. Less strict than ~해야 해요.

examples.en: Kids! You should say hello.
You should eat~ (gentle nudge)
Time to sleep now!

banmal_jondaemal.intro_ko: 이 에피소드는 반말과 존댓말의 차이가 아주 잘 보여요. 어른들끼리는 존댓말, 어른이 아이에게는 반말, 아이가 어른에게는 존댓말이에요.
banmal_jondaemal.intro_en: This episode shows the contrast beautifully. Adults speak formally to each other, adults speak casually to kids, and kids speak formally to adults.

rows.banmal: 안녕~ · 잘 있어. 한 번 놀러 와.

banmal_jondaemal.tip: 한국어에서 어른에게는 항상 존댓말이에요! 특히 처음 만나는 어른이나 부모님 친구에게는 꼭 존댓말을 써야 해요. · In Korean, always use formal speech with adults — especially parents' friends or people you're meeting for the first time.

adult_dialogue.intro_ko: 직장 동료가 집에 왔어요. 어른들끼리 존댓말로 대화해요.
adult_dialogue.intro_en: A work colleague comes to visit. Adults speaking formally with each other.

lines.speaker: A
lines.en: Welcome! So glad you could make it.

lines.speaker: B
lines.en: Thank you so much for having me.

lines.speaker: B
lines.text: 덕분에 잘 지내고 있어요. 박 선생님은요?
lines.en: Doing well, thanks. How about you, Teacher Park?

lines.speaker: A
lines.text: 저도 잘 지내고 있어요. 들어 오세요!
lines.en: I'm well too. Please come in!

usage.title: 호칭 — 이름 대신 관계로 부른다
usage.title_en: Address Terms — Calling by Relationship, Not Name
usage.explanation_en: In Korean culture, adults are rarely called by their first name. Instead, you use their relationship title or job title. This shows respect and is deeply rooted in Korean social culture.

examples.en: Ma'am, how much is this?
Thank you, sir!
Excuse me! (to get a server's attention in a restaurant)

usage.title: 어서 오세요 — 한국 어디서나
usage.explanation_en: You'll hear 어서 오세요 at restaurants, shops, and any time you walk into a business. It literally means 'come quickly / please come right in' and is the standard Korean welcome. Shopkeepers say it automatically when a customer enters.

examples.en: Welcome~ What can I get you?
Welcome! I'll get you a menu.

quiz.question_en: When do you use 어서 오세요?
options: 손님이 올 때 (when a guest arrives)
학교 갈 때 (when going to school)
quiz.explanation: 어서 오세요 = Welcome! 손님이 문 앞에 왔을 때 써요. · Used when a guest arrives at the door.
-셨어요 = honorific -었어요. 지내다 = to get along / to be doing. 어른에게 쓰는 높임말이에요.
덕분에 = thanks to you / owing to you. 상대방 덕분에 잘 지낸다는 따뜻한 표현이에요.
~해야지 = you should (gentle nudge). 인사해야지 = you should say hello! 부모님이 아이들한테 많이 써요.

quiz.question_en: Why did Liam ask 잘 있어요? (in formal speech)?
options: 미래 엄마는 어른이니까 — she's an adult
quiz.explanation: 미래 엄마는 어른이에요. 아이들은 어른에게 항상 존댓말을 써요! Kids always use 존댓말 with adults.

quiz.question_en: What does 한 번 놀러 와 mean?
quiz.explanation: 놀러 오다 = to come over (to hang out). 한 번 = sometime / once. 친한 사람에게 반말로 초대할 때 써요.

real_life.title: 한국 방문 예절
real_life.setup_ko: 한국에서 누군가의 집에 갈 때 알아두면 좋은 것들이에요.
real_life.setup_en: Good things to know when visiting someone's home in Korea.

lines.text: 선물을 가져가요 — 과일, 케이크, 음료수 등이 좋아요.
lines.en: Bring a small gift — fruit, cake, or drinks are popular.

lines.text: 신발을 벗고 들어가요! 한국 집에서는 필수예요.
lines.en: Take off your shoes at the door! This is a must in Korean homes.

lines.text: 음식을 권하면 처음엔 한 번 사양하는 게 예의예요.
lines.en: When food is offered, it's polite to decline once before accepting.
Always use two hands when giving or receiving from an elder.

real_life.title: 한국의 호칭 문화
real_life.setup_ko: 한국에서는 어른 이름을 직접 부르지 않아요 — 관계나 직함으로 불러요!
real_life.setup_en: In Korea, adults are rarely called by their first names — you use their relationship or title!

lines.en: Friend's mom → 아주머니 or '(friend's name)'s mom
Shop staff → 사장님 (boss) / 여기요! / 저기요! (Excuse me!)
Teachers are always called '선생님' — never by name.

lines.text: 직장에서 → 김 부장님, 이 과장님 (직함 + 님)
lines.en: At work → Director Kim, Manager Lee (title + 님)

real_life.title: 문화 팁 — ㅂ니다체 공식 인사
real_life.content_ko: 한국어에 공식 인사말이나 감사 표현에는 ㅂ니다 체를 쓰는 게 좋아요. '감사합니다', '잘 먹겠습니다', '축하합니다'처럼요. 특별한 자리, 어른 앞, 처음 만나는 사람에게 쓰면 예의 바른 인상을 줄 수 있어요.
real_life.content_en: For formal greetings or expressions of gratitude, use the ㅂ니다 style: 감사합니다 (thank you), 잘 먹겠습니다 (I'll eat well / bon appétit), 축하합니다 (congratulations). Use it in special situations, with elders, or when meeting someone for the first time.

pronunciation.title: 비음화 — ㅂ + ㄴ → [ㅁ+ㄴ]
pronunciation.rule: 받침 ㅂ 뒤에 ㄴ이 오면 [ㅁ]으로 발음해요
pronunciation.tip: ㅂ 받침은 ㄴ, ㅁ 앞에서 [ㅁ]으로 바뀌어요. 감사합니다 → [감사함니다]로 자연스럽게 발음해요!

pronunciation.title: 셨어요 발음
pronunciation.tip: 셨어요는 [셔써요]로 빠르게 발음해요. 'syeo-sseo-yo' — 한 번에 말하면 자연스러워요!

self_check.en: I know the difference between 어서 오세요 and 들어 오세요.
I can politely ask an adult using -셨어요.

self_check.ko: '덕분에 잘 지내고 있어요'로 인사할 수 있어요.
self_check.en: I can greet someone with 덕분에 잘 지내고 있어요.
I understand the difference between ~고 있어요 and ~고 계세요.

self_check.ko: 아주머니, 아저씨 등 한국 호칭을 알아요.
self_check.en: I know Korean address terms like 아주머니 and 아저씨.
